1: Mark's original ending at 16:8 (they said nothing to anyone, for they were afraid) is among the most discussed textual questions in NT scholarship. The longer ending (16:9-20) is absent from the oldest manuscripts (Sinaiticus, Vaticanus) and contains vocabulary and style not found in Mark 1-15. The shorter ending is attested by Eusebius and Jerome as the best manuscript tradition. The abrupt ending at 16:8 may be deliberate: Mark's Gospel begins with abrupt urgency and ends with the open invitation of an unanswered question. The resurrection is announced but not narratively resolved — the reader is placed in the position of the women: what will you do with the empty tomb and the promise that the risen Lord goes before you?
2: "Very early on the first day of the week, just after sunrise" — "first day of the week" (mia tōn sabbatōn) is the Jewish designation for Sunday (the day after the Sabbath). The early church adopted this day as the Lord's Day (kyriakē hēmera, Rev 1:10; Didache 14.1: "On the Lord's Day, gather together") because of the resurrection, eventually distinguishing it from Sabbath observance. The specific detail "just after sunrise" (anateilan­tos tou hēliou) combines urgency (they came as early as they could) with a subtle contrast to the darkness at Jesus's death (15:33): the resurrection is associated with the return of light.
3: "Who will roll the stone away from the entrance of the tomb?" — the practical concern about the stone reflects the reality of the burial Joseph had arranged (15:46): the rolling stone was a substantial disc, requiring several people to move. The women's question as they walk shows that they went to the tomb expecting to anoint a body behind a sealed entrance, without any expectation of resurrection. Their concern about access is the concern of those who believe the story ends with burial. The question they ask on the way is answered immediately when they arrive — the stone is already moved.
4: "They saw that the stone, which was very large, had been rolled away" — the stone had been moved before their arrival; Mark does not explain by whom or how (contrast Matt 28:2, which describes an angel rolling it away at the moment of resurrection). The stone was "very large" (sphodra megas) — Mark emphasizes its size, underscoring that moving it required significant force. The passive voice ("had been rolled away") attributes the action to divine agency without specifying it. The women find not the closed tomb they expected but an opened one — the first sign that something other than ordinary burial has occurred.
5: "A young man dressed in a white robe sitting on the right side" — Mark does not use the word "angel" (angelos) here; the young man (neaniskos) in white is identified as angelic by context. White garments in Jewish apocalyptic literature consistently identified angelic or heavenly beings (Dan 7:9; 10:5; 2 Macc 11:8; 1 Enoch 71:1). The right side was the position of honor and authority. "They were alarmed" (exethambēthēsan — a strong compound: deeply startled, struck with astonishment) — the same root appears at 9:15 and 14:33; it is a response appropriate to an encounter with the numinous.
6: "Don't be alarmed. You are looking for Jesus the Nazarene, who was crucified. He has risen! He is not here. See the place where they laid him." — the angel's proclamation is the first Easter announcement in the Gospel tradition. Four elements: a command to stop being afraid (conventional angelic formula: cf. Dan 10:12; Luke 1:13,30; 2:10), identification of the one they seek (Jesus of Nazareth, the crucified one — the identification is historically specific: not a vision or a spirit but the same person who was crucified), the proclamation of resurrection ("he has risen," ēgerthē — divine passive: God raised him), and an invitation to see the empty tomb as evidence. The proclamation grounds the resurrection claim in historical specificity.
7: "But go, tell his disciples and Peter" — Peter's specific naming after his three-fold denial (14:66-72) is the resurrection's first act of restoration. The angel singles out Peter by name — he is not excluded from the community despite his failure; he is specifically summoned. The rendezvous instruction ("He is going ahead of you into Galilee. There you will see him, just as he told you") fulfills 14:28 ("after I have risen, I will go ahead of you into Galilee"). The return to Galilee is not retreat but recommissioning: the ministry that began in Galilee (1:14) will be reconstituted there. Paul's tradition (1 Cor 15:5) confirms that Peter was among the first resurrection witnesses.
8: "Trembling and bewildered, the women went out and fled from the tomb. They said nothing to anyone, because they were afraid" (ephobounto gar — "for they were afraid") — the original ending of Mark. This ending is attested by Codex Sinaiticus, Codex Vaticanus, and most early witnesses; Eusebius and Jerome note that almost all Greek copies they knew ended here. The abruptness puzzled ancient readers (which is why vv.9-20 were added), but it is consistent with Mark's literary style and theology. The women's silence and fear are not a final statement of failure but a literary technique: the reader now holds the news the women could not speak; the Gospel ends with an invitation — the story does not end here; it continues with the reader who has received the proclamation the women could not yet speak.
9: Mark 16:9-20, the "Long Ending," is absent from Codex Sinaiticus (א) and Codex Vaticanus (B), the oldest substantially complete Greek manuscripts of the NT, as well as from Clement of Alexandria, Origen, Eusebius, and Jerome's testimony about the manuscripts they consulted. The Long Ending is present in the Western text (D) and the Byzantine majority text, but its textual history indicates it was added in the second century to provide a more satisfying conclusion. The material in vv.9-20 is not invented — it represents authentic early traditions about Jesus's resurrection appearances (Mary Magdalene's encounter, the Emmaus disciples, the commissioning, the ascension) — but the question is whether it was part of Mark's original Gospel.
10: Mary Magdalene goes and tells those who had been with Jesus — "mourning and weeping" (penthousin kai klaiousin). The grief described is consistent with the disciples' post-crucifixion state: they have witnessed (or heard of) the death of the one they believed would redeem Israel (Luke 24:21). Mary's report is the first resurrection testimony in the Long Ending's sequence. The pattern of announcement, disbelief, and subsequent appearance (vv.10-14) mirrors the pattern in Luke 24 and John 20, suggesting the Long Ending is drawing on the other Gospel traditions.
11: "When they heard that Jesus was alive and that she had seen him, they did not believe it" (ēpistēsan) — the disciples' initial disbelief of Mary Magdalene's testimony is consistent across all four Gospel resurrection accounts (Luke 24:11: "But they did not believe the women, because their words seemed to them like nonsense"). In Jewish law, women's testimony was legally inferior to men's in many contexts (m. Shevuot 4.1). The disciples' disbelief is also psychologically realistic: the crucifixion had comprehensively destroyed their expectation, and resurrection from the dead was outside their framework of expectation even after Jesus's predictions.
12: "Afterward Jesus appeared in a different form to two of them while they were walking in the country" — this corresponds to the Emmaus road appearance of Luke 24:13-35, where two disciples traveling to Emmaus encounter the risen Jesus but do not initially recognize him ("their eyes were prevented from recognizing him," Luke 24:16). "In a different form" (en heterai morphē) is the Long Ending's compact summary of what Luke narrates at length: the risen Jesus is not always immediately recognizable in his resurrection appearances, a consistent feature of the appearance accounts (cf. John 20:14-15, Mary Magdalene does not recognize him; John 21:4, the disciples do not recognize him).
13: "These returned and reported it to the rest; but they did not believe them either" — the continuing disbelief accumulates through three failed testimony sequences: Mary Magdalene (v.11), the two Emmaus travelers (v.13), and finally the appearance to the Eleven themselves (v.14) ends the chain. The pattern of repeated disbelief before the final confronting appearance serves a historical apologetic purpose: the resurrection faith did not arise from credulity or wishful thinking but from appearances that overcame systematic disbelief among the very people who would have most wanted to believe but could not.
14: "Later Jesus appeared to the Eleven as they were eating; he rebuked them for their lack of faith and their stubborn refusal to believe those who had seen him after he had risen" — the appearance to the Eleven corresponds to Luke 24:36-43 and John 20:19-23. The rebuke for "stubborn refusal to believe" (sklērokardia, hardness of heart — the same term Jesus used to rebuke the disciples' failure to understand the feedings, 8:17) establishes that the resurrection witnesses were not naive believers but resistant skeptics who required confrontation with the physical reality of the risen Christ before they would believe.
15: "Go into all the world and preach the gospel to all creation" — the Great Commission of the Long Ending (corresponding to Matt 28:19-20, Luke 24:46-49, John 20:21-23, Acts 1:8). "All creation" (pasē tē ktisei) is broader than Matthew's "all nations" — it includes the entire created order as the scope of the gospel's reach. The commission's context in Gentile-oriented Mark makes the "all the world" language particularly resonant: the Gentile Roman audience for whom Mark was written is included in the "all creation" that the gospel addresses.
16: "Whoever believes and is baptized will be saved, but whoever does not believe will be condemned" — the connection of belief with baptism reflects early Christian practice (Acts 2:38, 8:12-13, 16:31-33) where baptism was the immediate public expression of belief and the act of incorporation into the community. The negative statement concerns only unbelief ("whoever does not believe") — not absence of baptism. The verse is not establishing baptism as independently necessary for salvation but as the expected outward expression of the belief that saves; the condemnation comes from unbelief, not from unbaptized status.
17: "And these signs will accompany those who believe: In my name they will drive out demons; they will speak in new tongues" — the promised signs correspond to documented practices in the early church: exorcism is a consistent feature of Acts (5:16, 8:7, 16:18, 19:12) and Paul's letters (1 Cor 12:10 lists "distinguishing between spirits"); speaking in new tongues (glōssais lalēsousin kainais) appears at Pentecost (Acts 2:4), Caesarea (Acts 10:46), Ephesus (Acts 19:6), and is discussed at length in 1 Corinthians 12-14. These promises in the Long Ending reflect the actual experience of the early Gentile mission.
18: "They will pick up snakes with their hands; and when they drink deadly poison, it will not hurt them at all; they will place their hands on sick people, and they will get well" — the snake-handling promise echoes Luke 10:19 ("I have given you authority to tread on snakes and scorpions") and is illustrated accidentally in Acts 28:3-6 (Paul is bitten by a viper on Malta but experiences no harm). The healing of the sick by hand-laying is consistent with the practice throughout Acts (3:7, 9:17, 28:8). The poison immunity is not otherwise illustrated in the NT but appears in early Christian tradition (Eusebius HE 3.39.9 reports a similar claim about the Apostle John).
19: "After the Lord Jesus had spoken to them, he was taken up into heaven and he sat at the right hand of God" — the Ascension is narrated in Luke 24:50-51 and Acts 1:9-11. "Sat at the right hand of God" is the language of Psalm 110:1 ("The LORD says to my lord: 'Sit at my right hand until I make your enemies a footstool for your feet'") — the Psalm Jesus quoted at his trial (14:62: "you will see the Son of Man sitting at the right hand of the Mighty One"). The Ascension and session at the right hand is the fulfillment of the Danielic promise (Dan 7:13-14) that Jesus claimed before the Sanhedrin: the vindicated Son of Man enthroned in the presence of the Ancient of Days.
20: "Then the disciples went out and preached everywhere, and the Lord worked with them and confirmed his word by the signs that accompanied it" — the Long Ending closes with a summary that corresponds to the book of Acts as a whole: the disciples' mission to "everywhere" (Acts 1-28 traces this from Jerusalem to Rome), and the Lord's confirming presence through signs (Acts 2:43, 3:1-10, 5:12-16, 8:6-7, 14:3). The Long Ending does not end the Gospel with an ending but with a beginning: the mission that Mark's Gospel narrates (1:1: "the beginning of the good news of Jesus Christ") continues in the preaching of those who witnessed the resurrection and received the commission to take the gospel to all creation.
